content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/2511.03080
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/2511.03080/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/2511.03080

Begin by reading paper.md.

name: Read
content:
1	## PolyNorm: Few-Shot LLM-Based Text Normalization for Text-to-Speech
     2	
     3	Michel Wong * , Ali Alshehri, Sophia Kao, Haotian He
     4	
     5	{ michel.wong, a alshehri, sophia kao, haotian } @apple.com
     6	
     7	Apple
     8	
     9	scarcity of resources, TN systems must also handle contextual ambiguities that arise within or across languages. The same surface form can be normalized differently depending on both the linguistic context and the target language, as illustrated in Figure 1 1 .
    10	
    11	Figure 1: The ambiguous token 17° is normalized differently based on context and language.
    12	
    13	<!-- image -->
    14	
    15	Later development lead to TN systems that are based on statistical and hybrid data-driven approaches (Grali´ nski et al., 2006; Zhu et al., 2007; Sproat and Jaitly, 2016). For example, Grali´ nski et al. (2006) treated TN as a machine translation problem, mapping textual representations into their spoken equivalents, while Zhu et al. (2007) introduced a unified tagging approach using Conditional Random Fields (CRF) to simultaneously handle multiple TN subtasks, emphasizing their interdependencies. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown great potential in addressing these limitations by leveraging their broad linguistic knowledge. Zhang et al. (2024) used GPT models to perform text normalization in few-shot scenarios, incorporating self-consistency reasoning and linguistically informed prompt engineering, achieving error rates approximately 40% lower than those of production-level WFST systems.
    16	
    17	Building on prior research that focused on English and GPT models, this work introduces PolyNorm, an LLM-assisted TN framework that leverages prompting and contextual learning, and com-
    18	
    19	1 ' ` E il 17° secolo.' translates to 'It's the 17 th
    20	
    21	century.' 1 'Estoy en el 17º piso.' translates to 'I am on the 17 th floor.'
    22	
    23	## Abstract
    24	
    25	Text Normalization (TN) is a key preprocessing step in Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems, converting written forms into their canonical spoken equivalents. Traditional TN systems can exhibit high accuracy, but involve substantial engineering effort, are difficult to scale, and pose challenges to language coverage, particularly in low-resource settings. We propose PolyNorm, a prompt-based approach to TN using Large Language Models (LLMs), aiming to reduce the reliance on manually crafted rules and enable broader linguistic applicability with minimal human intervention. Additionally, we present a language-agnostic pipeline for automatic data curation and evaluation, designed to facilitate scalable experimentation across diverse languages. Experiments across eight languages show consistent reductions in the word error rate (WER) compared to a productiongrade-based system. To support further research, we release PolyNorm-Benchmark, a multilingual data set covering a diverse range of text normalization phenomena.
    26	
    27	## 1 Introduction
    28	
    29	TN transforms written input, often dense with numbers, abbreviations, and special characters, into fluent speech-friendly text. Early TN systems relied primarily on rule-based approaches using weighted finite-state transducers (WFSTs) (Sproat et al., 2001). Although such systems have historically achieved high accuracy, they depend heavily on manual rules and extensive human verification, making them expensive and time-consuming to develop and maintain. These challenges are even more pronounced when dealing with low-resource or morphologically rich languages, such as Arabic and Polish (Mosquera et al., 2012; Po´ swiata and Perełkiewicz, 2019), where linguistic diversity complicates the creation of rules. Beyond the pares multiple LLMs across diverse languages. Our research pursues two primary goals: (1) developing a sustainable, cost-effective TN solution with minimal human involvement, and (2) creating a reliable, language-agnostic process for automatic data curation and evaluation applicable across both high- and low-resource languages, offering a scalable and accessible TN solution for modern TTS systems.
    30	
    31	* Correspondence: michel.wong@apple.com
    32	
    33	## 2 Data
    34	
    35	Our research focuses on American English, German, French, Mexican Spanish, Italian, Lithuanian, Japanese, and Mandarin Chinese.
    36	
    37	We start with a subset of the English Kestrel dataset (Ebden and Sproat, 2015) comprising 14 Kestrel categories extracted from the first file of the dataset, which contains approximately 880,000 lines. For English, to construct a balanced subset, we select a total of 1,400 test cases, removing all sentences that lack normalization targets and retaining the first 100 examples per category from the Kestrel dataset for benchmarking purposes. We initially experimented with translating both the original and normalized English texts into the target languages with NLLB-200 model (Facebook AI, 2022). However, we found most translated texts unsuitable as ground truth due to inconsistent quality, translation and inadequate localization of the normalized forms. In addition, the limited categories do not fully cover the scope of normalization phenomena that we aim to evaluate. Therefore, instead of using the entire subset for benchmarking, we reserved some of the higher-quality data, reviewed and verified by language experts, as part of the prompt examples, which will be discussed in the Methodology section.
    38	
    39	To address these challenges, we use DeepSeekR1 (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) to generate datasets comprising unnormalized and normalized text pairs as our new multilingual benchmark suite, PolyNormBenchmark 2 , designed to ensure consistency across languages and taxonomies. Each dataset spans 27 normalization categories (listed in Table 1) built on Kestrel categories and expanded with new classes, comprising 20 examples per category for a total of 540 high-quality data points per language. For Chinese and Japanese, we define abbreviations as truncated forms or initial-letter substitutions, since conventional definitions may not fully capture their
    40	
    41	2 https://github.com/apple/ml-speech-polynorm-bench
    42	
    43	## List of Text Normalization Categories
    44	
    45	Table 1: List of 27 text normalization categories used for multilingual data construction.
    46	
    47	| Cardinal Decimal Fraction Currency Address ISBN Roman Numeral Sports Score Symbol Chemical Formula Vehicle/Product Code Version Number Musical Notation Electronic (URL/Email)   | Date Ordinal Time Unit (Measure) Acronym/Initialism Biological Classification Telephone Mathematical Expression Abbreviations Legal Reference Geographic Coordinates License/Serial Number Stock Ticker   |
    48	|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
    49	
    50	use in conversational language.
    51	
    52	The initial outputs of DeepSeek serve as a foundation, then each example is subsequently edited and verified by internal language experts to ensure the highest standards of linguistic precision, naturalness, and overall quality. The orthography and formatting of synthetic data follow the conventions of target languages. For example, in French, decimal separator in currency is written with a comma instead of a period, with the currency symbol after the number:
    53	
    54	Orig:
    55	
    56	Le taux de change est de 1 = C = 1,10$.
    57	
    58	Norm: ´ egale un virgule dix dollars.
    59	
    60	Le taux de change est de un euro
    61	
    62	Orig:
    63	
    64	El 1º de Enero.
    65	
    66	Norm:
    67	
    68	El primero de Enero.
    69	
    70	Orig:
    71	
    72	容 量 は 500mL 。
    73	
    74	Norm:
    75	
    76	ヨウリョウはゴヒャクミリリット
    77	
    78	ル。
    79	
    80	## 3 Methodology
    81	
    82	We define a normalization error as any output in which the normalized text deviates from the expected conventional spoken form. For example, rendering 'Dr' as 'Doctor' instead of 'Drive' in an address, or normalizing '12:30' as 'twelve thirty AM' when the time format is ambiguous.
    83	
    84	For clarity and consistency, we standardize normalization to common conventions across languages when applicable. For instance, in American English, we follow the month-day-year format, consistent with our TTS production system expectations. Years like '2020' are normalized as twenty twenty , and dates such as '4/18' are normalized as april eighteenth .
    85	
    86	Orig:
    87	
    88	05/20/2023
    89	
    90	Norm (accepted): may twentieth twenty twenty three
    91	
    92	Norm (rejected):
    93	
    94	the twentieth of may two
    95	
    96	thousand
    97	
    98	and twenty three
    99	
   100	Certain differences are not considered errors in our evaluation, as they do not affect the semantic or phonetic realization of the text. These include casing, optional spacing (e.g., in German compound words), and orthographic variants such as 'ß' vs. 'ss' in specific German contexts, ensuring the evaluation focuses on differences affecting meaning, pronunciation, or intelligibility. To identify such issues and iteratively refine our system, we collaborate with language experts who review LLM outputs on our development sets and flag inaccuracies or inconsistencies, which inform benchmark corrections, in-context learning (ICL) examples refinements, and system iterations.
   101	
   102	We adopt a few-shot prompting strategy using ICL examples to guide the LLM in performing text normalization across all languages. This approach allows the model to generalize from a small number of curated examples across various categories, from numerical expressions to acronyms, eliminating extensive fine-tuning or rule-based engineering.
   103	
   104	Our prompt design follows a structured, step-bystep and language-agnostic format composed of three components: 1) Instruction Prompt , which defines the normalization task, 2) In-Context Learning Examples , which demonstrate how different types of text should be normalized, and 3) Target Unnormalized Input , to which the model applies learned patterns. PolyNorm uses a standardized English instruction prompt with localized ICL examples tailored to each language's linguistic and stylistic norms.
   105	
   106	## 3.1 ICL Examples
   107	
   108	Few-shot prompting with ICL offers a lightweight, scalable solution for multilingual text normalization, relying on high-quality, language-specific examples. The effectiveness of this approach depends on the quality and relevance of the ICL examples provided to the model. We use a unified prompt format across eight languages, varying only the localized examples-except for Japanese, where a supplementary prompt guides katakana output. Our curated ICL set, excluded from PolyNorm-Bench, includes 80-100 high-quality machine-translated Kestrel data and DeepSeek synthetic examples (e.g., favoring 'twenty nineteen' over 'two thou- sand nineteen' for '2019' in English), each normalized and validated by experts to ensure stylistic consistency across domains.
   109	
   110	## 3.2 Error Analysis and Hillclimbing Iteration
   111	
   112	We analyzed discrepancies between expert-verified development sets and LLM outputs from GPT models to identify systematic weaknesses. Common errors included incorrect numeral expansions and inconsistent handling of language-specific formats such as date and currency. To address these issues, we identified categories or patterns where the model underperformed, then revised or added ICL examples focused on these error types, improving the model's output with each iteration. This feedback loop refined normalization via prompt tuning and better examples, reducing errors and improving cross-lingual consistency.
   113	
   114	## 4 Results
   115	
   116	We evaluated GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4o as candidate systems against Apple Siri production rulebased normalization baseline system. Because it is proprietary, we cannot release code or provide external access. However, to help readers judge strength and fairness, we report baseline scores for a representative subset of seven categories across all eight languages in Table 4 below 3 . It is important to note that the baseline predates our benchmark and was not tuned to it.
   117	
   118	All systems were tested on PolyNorm across eight languages listed in Table 2. We report the overall performance with Word Error Rate (WER) and BLEU, which reflects how closely the model's output matches the expected spoken form and relative surface similarity to the reference respectively. For non-whitespace languages (Chinese and Japanese), we report Character Error Rate instead of WER to avoid conflating tokenization errors with true normalization errors. PolyNorm demonstrates substantial advantages in terms of flexibility, coverage, and the ability to handle previously intractable normalization challenges with minimal manual human intervention. Results in Table 2 show improvements in both WER and BLEU across the two LLM systems compared to the rule-based baseline. GPT4o achieves a WER ranging from 4.17% to 7.88% for all languages.
   119	
   120	We observe that domain-specific constructs, such as website URLs and usernames, which rely on static rules, often pose challenges for tokenization and rule-based normalization due to their inherent unpredictability and rapid evolution. PolyNorm addresses these challenges with the contextual capabilities of LLMs to intelligently segment such expressions. For instance, https://www.mediacityuk.co.uk is normalized as h t t p s colon slash slash media city u k dot co dot u k .
   121	
   122	3 Table 3 in Appendix B shows side-by-side examples illustrating some incorrect baseline outputs
   123	
   124	Table 2: WER and BLEU scores across 8 languages using PolyNorm-Benchmark. Best scores per row are in bold.
   125	
   126	| Language         | Baseline   | Baseline   | GPT-4o-mini   | GPT-4o-mini   | GPT-4o   | GPT-4o   |
   127	|------------------|------------|------------|---------------|---------------|----------|----------|
   128	|                  | WER(%)     | BLEU (%)   | WER(%)        | BLEU (%)      | WER(%)   | BLEU (%) |
   129	| German           | 10.74      | 60.83      | 7.18          | 78.35         | 4.17     | 84.92    |
   130	| American English | 9.84       | 70.84      | 6.60          | 85.02         | 4.28     | 89.85    |
   131	| Mexican Spanish  | 11.92      | 55.74      | 11.19         | 62.49         | 7.69     | 71.90    |
   132	| French           | 9.72       | 69.02      | 7.70          | 79.84         | 5.65     | 86.18    |
   133	| Italian          | 15.02      | 55.14      | 8.33          | 72.81         | 4.56     | 84.96    |
   134	| Lithuanian       | 10.04      | 67.12      | 10.54         | 64.36         | 6.99     | 73.96    |
   135	| Mandarin Chinese | 11.36      | 69.63      | 6.65          | 79.04         | 5.05     | 83.11    |
   136	| Japanese         | 17.49      | 47.76      | 10.78         | 68.32         | 7.88     | 77.89    |
   137	
   138	Another observation of PolyNorm's context awareness over rule-based systems is its handling of dashes between numbers. In sports scores, a dash is typically verbalized as 'to' (e.g., '3-2' as 'three to two'), whereas in phone numbers it is ignored and the digits are read individually. A rulebased system would require an explicit and often verbose set of context-specific rules to make this distinction, while PolyNorm can infer the correct interpretation directly from context, even in novel or ambiguous formats.
   139	
   140	## 5 Discussion
   141	
   142	## 5.1 Impact
   143	
   144	Training a reliable TTS system can require millions of high-quality data points. While effective in controlled environments, traditional TN pipelines, heavily rule-based, demand ongoing annotation and patching, often taking months per language and struggling to generalize or scale to noisy, ambiguous, or evolving inputs. PolyNorm replaces this manual loop with an LLM-driven framework using prompt tuning and in-context learning, cutting development overhead and accelerating iteration by refining prompts or examples.
   145	
   146	Crowdsourcing normalization can be expensive, and it takes weeks of review cycles. PolyNorm, combined with few-shot in-context learning, achieves high grapheme-to-phoneme accuracy with far fewer labeled examples, lowering the barrier for TN in low-resource languages. Beyond cost and efficiency gains, LLM-based TN offers more consistent outputs, better generalization, and language-agnostic deployment, while allowing localized examples to boost performance. By reducing manual effort and speeding iteration, PolyNorm lets developers focus on quality and localization, accelerating research and production.
   147	
   148	## 5.2 Future Work
   149	
   150	Future work for PolyNorm includes enabling diacritization restoration in languages like Arabic and Hebrew, where diacritics serve critical grammatical and phonetic roles, and missing markers affect grammar and pronunciation. Targeted prompting could improve accuracy and naturalness of speech synthesis. Japanese and other non-whitespace languages could benefit from integrated tokenizers and multitask learning. While normalizing input to Katakana resolves homograph ambiguities (e.g., 〇 read as 'rei,' 'zero,' or 'maru') in Japanese, it may disrupt pitch accent patterns crucial for naturalness. Future work should incorporate suprasegmental features like pitch accent and tone prediction into the normalization pipeline.
   151	
   152	## 6 Conclusion
   153	
   154	We present PolyNorm, a multilingual, LLMassisted text normalization system designed to address scalability, cost, and language-coverage limitations of rule-based systems. It achieves strong normalization performance across languages and categories, and significantly reduces WER compared to production-grade rule-based systems, demonstrating its robustness and adaptability.
   155	
   156	Beyond performance, PolyNorm represents a paradigm shift in how normalization systems can be developed, maintained, and scaled. The use of instruct prompting and in-context learning enables rapid iteration cycles, eliminates the dependency on handcrafted rules, and lowers the entry barrier for low-resource languages and domain-specific applications. Our language-agnostic prompt design and PolyNorm-Benchmark further establish a foundation for standardized evaluation and future research in LLM-based normalization. As we look ahead, expanding our system to handle complex linguistic phenomena such as diacritization, non-whitespace tokenization, and suprasegmental features will further broaden its applicability, making high-quality TTS systems accessible across a wider range of languages and user contexts.
   157	
   158	## 7 Acknowledgement
   159	
   160	We are grateful to Alistair Conkie and Xu Shao for their thorough review and valuable feedback that significantly improved this research.
   161	
   162	## 8 Ethical Consideration
   163	
   164	## 8.1 Lay Summary
   165	
   166	This project explores using large language models (LLMs) to automate text normalization-a key step in processing raw text such as speech transcripts, handwriting, or informal writing. Text normalization standardizes text by handling tasks from formatting dates and numbers to restoring diacritics in accented languages. Our LLM-based system offers a scalable, efficient alternative by combining raw input with tailored prompts and few-shot examples. This approach improves consistency and quality across multiple languages, making it wellsuited for applications like text-to-speech, machine translation, and speech recognition.
   167	
   168	## 8.2 AI Safety Disclosure
   169	
   170	LLM-based normalization introduces ethical and safety risks. Because the model learns from large datasets, it may replicate biases in the training data. For example, in gendered languages like Arabic, ambiguous inputs such as 'ant' (which could mean 'you [masc.]' or 'you [fem.]') might be resolved in biased ways. If unaddressed, such biases can reinforce stereotypes or generate inappropriate outputs. Inaccurate normalization may also mislead learners, reinforce incorrect usage, and reduce trust in downstream systems. Therefore, while PolyNorm offers efficient, multilingual normalization, it must be developed with careful attention to data quality, bias mitigation, and user safety to ensure ethical deployment.
   171	
   172	## 9 Limitations
   173	
   174	While PolyNorm demonstrates strong potential in terms of efficiency and multilingual consistency, the model's performance is highly dependent on the quality and representativeness of data used as in-context learning examples. Categories covered in the examples may not capture the full range of normalization needs across different languages and varieties. Furthermore, errors in normalization can propagate into downstream tasks such as translation, sentiment analysis, or educational tools. Although expert-reviewed examples were used for in-context learning, the model may still struggle with edge cases or uncommon linguistic patterns. These limitations must be addressed before considering the system for widespread or critical work.
   175	
   176	## References
   177	
   178	DeepSeek-AI. 2025. Deepseek-r1: Incentivizing reasoning capability in LLMs via reinforcement learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.12948 .
   179	
   180	Peter Ebden and Richard Sproat. 2015. The Kestrel TTS text normalization system. Natural Language Engineering , 21(3):333-353.
   181	
   182	Facebook AI. 2022. Nllb-200 distilled (1.3b). https://huggingface.co/facebook/ nllb-200-distilled-1.3B .
   183	
   184	Filip Grali´ nski, Krzysztof Jassem, Agnieszka Wagner, and Mikołaj Wypych. 2006. Text normalization as a special case of machine translation. In Proceedings of the International Multiconference on Computer Science and Information Technology , pages 51-56.
   185	
   186	Alejandro Mosquera, Elena Lloret, and Paloma Moreda. 2012. Towards facilitating the accessibility of web 2.0 texts through text normalisation. In Proceedings of the First Workshop on Speech and Language Processing for Assistive Technologies , pages 9-17.
   187	
   188	Rafał Po´ swiata and Michał Perełkiewicz. 2019. Numbers normalisation in the inflected languages: a case study of polish. In Proceedings of the 7th Workshop on Balto-Slavic Natural Language Processing , pages 23-28.
   189	
   190	Richard Sproat, Alan W Black, Stanley Chen, Shankar Kumar, Mari Ostendorf, and Christine D Richards. 2001. Normalization of non-standard words. Computer Speech and Language , 15(3):287-333.
   191	
   192	Richard Sproat and Navdeep Jaitly. 2016. Rnn approaches to text normalization: A challenge. arXiv preprint arXiv:1611.00068 .
   193	
   194	Yang Zhang, Travis M. Bartley, Mariana GraterolFuenmayor, Vitaly Lavrukhin, Evelina Bakhturina, and Boris Ginsburg. 2024. A chat about boring problems: Studying gpt-based text normalization. Preprint , arXiv:2309.13426.
   195	
   196	Conghui Zhu, Jie Tang, Hang Li, Hwee Tou Ng, and Tiejun Zhao. 2007. A unified tagging approach to text normalization. In Proceedings of the 45th annual meeting of the association of computational linguistics , pages 688-695.
   197	
   198	## Appendix
   199	
   200	## A Instruction Prompt Template
   201	
   202	This section presents the instruction prompt template. Note that certain locales include supplementary prompts tailored to their linguistic or stylistic characteristics. In-context learning (ICL) examples are omitted here for brevity.
   203	
   204	You are an accurate text normalizer for { locale } . Your task is to normalize unstandardized text from the following categories to truly reflects how the real speech is, based on the context:
   205	
   206	- Cardinal
   207	- Date
   208	- Decimal
   209	- Ordinal
   210	- Fraction
   211	- Time
   212	- Currency
   213	- Unit (Measure)
   214	- Electronic Address (URL or Email)
   215	- Initialism or Acronym
   216	- ISBN
   217	- Roman Numeral
   218	- Telephone
   219	- Sports Score
   220	- Mathematical Expression
   221	- Symbol
   222	- Abbreviation
   223	- Chemical Formula
   224	- Legal Reference
   225	- Vehicle or Product Code
   226	- Geographic Coordinates
   227	- Version Number
   228	- License Plate or Serial Number
   229	- Musical Notation
   230	- Stock Ticker
   231	- Biological Classification
   232	- Address
   233	- Other unnormalized text
   234	
   235	Some important rules:
   236	
   237	- When normalizing acronyms, spell out to their full forms for clarity, except when the acronym is a widely recognized and pronounceable name (e.g. 'NASA' or 'NASCAR'). In those cases, keep the acronym as-is and pronounce it as a word.
   238	
   239	- If the acronym combines a letter and a word, split accordingly.
   240	- Convert punctuation that is spoken aloud into words. For example, write 'dot' instead of a period in URLs and emails.
   241	- To ensure clarity, segment compound words, websites and file names into recognizable component words rather than keeping them as a whole word.
   242	- Symbols in a file name should be read as is.
   243	- Common file extensions (.jpeg, .jpg, .txt, etc) should be spoken out. Uncommon file extensions should be spelled out.
   244	
   245	## B Baseline Normalization Examples
   246	
   247	Table 3: Examples of baseline TN errors across the targeted languages and selected categories.
   248	
   249	| Language           | Category                   | Example                                          | Ground Truth                                                          | Error                                                                                          |
   250	|--------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
   251	| German             | Address                    | Die Wohnung befindet sich in der Hauptstraße 45. | die wohnung befindet sich in der hauptstrasse f¨ unfundvierzig.       | die wohnung befindet sich in der hauptstrasse f¨ unf und vierzig.                              |
   252	| American En- glish | Legal References           | The regulation is 15 CFR Part 12.                | the regulation is fifteen c f r part twelve.                          | the regulation is fifteen cfr part twelve.                                                     |
   253	| Mexican Spanish    | Phone Numbers              | Mi celular es 442-789- 0123.                     | mi celular es cuatro cuatro dos, siete ocho nueve, cero uno dos tres. | mi celular es cuatrocientos cuarenta y dos, setecientos ochenta y nueve, cero un veintitr´ es. |
   254	| French             | Sports Scores              | Le tennis de table a fini 11- 9.                 | le tennis de table a fini onze ` a neuf.                              | le tennis de table a fini onze moins neuf.                                                     |
   255	| Italian            | Currencies                 | La tariffa ` e CHF 20.                           | la tariffa ` e venti franchi svizzeri.                                | la tariffa ` e c h f venti.                                                                    |
   256	| Lithuanian         | Mathematical Ex- pressions | 5 × 6 = 30.                                      | penki kart ˇ seˇ si lygu trisdeˇ simt.                                | penki kart ˇ seˇ si yra trisdeˇ simt.                                                          |

name: Grep
content:
100:Certain differences are not considered errors in our evaluation, as they do not affect the semantic or phonetic realization of the text. These include casing, optional spacing (e.g., in German compound words), and orthographic variants such as 'ß' vs. 'ss' in specific German contexts, ensuring the evaluation focuses on differences affecting meaning, pronunciation, or intelligibility. To identify such issues and iteratively refine our system, we collaborate with language experts who review LLM outputs on our development sets and flag inaccuracies or inconsistencies, which inform benchmark corrections, in-context learning (ICL) examples refinements, and system iterations.
102:We adopt a few-shot prompting strategy using ICL examples to guide the LLM in performing text normalization across all languages. This approach allows the model to generalize from a small number of curated examples across various categories, from numerical expressions to acronyms, eliminating extensive fine-tuning or rule-based engineering.
104:Our prompt design follows a structured, step-bystep and language-agnostic format composed of three components: 1) Instruction Prompt , which defines the normalization task, 2) In-Context Learning Examples , which demonstrate how different types of text should be normalized, and 3) Target Unnormalized Input , to which the model applies learned patterns. PolyNorm uses a standardized English instruction prompt with localized ICL examples tailored to each language's linguistic and stylistic norms.
106:## 3.1 ICL Examples
108:Few-shot prompting with ICL offers a lightweight, scalable solution for multilingual text normalization, relying on high-quality, language-specific examples. The effectiveness of this approach depends on the quality and relevance of the ICL examples provided to the model. We use a unified prompt format across eight languages, varying only the localized examples-except for Japanese, where a supplementary prompt guides katakana output. Our curated ICL set, excluded from PolyNorm-Bench, includes 80-100 high-quality machine-translated Kestrel data and DeepSeek synthetic examples (e.g., favoring 'twenty nineteen' over 'two thou- sand nineteen' for '2019' in English), each normalized and validated by experts to ensure stylistic consistency across domains.
112:We analyzed discrepancies between expert-verified development sets and LLM outputs from GPT models to identify systematic weaknesses. Common errors included incorrect numeral expansions and inconsistent handling of language-specific formats such as date and currency. To address these issues, we identified categories or patterns where the model underperformed, then revised or added ICL examples focused on these error types, improving the model's output with each iteration. This feedback loop refined normalization via prompt tuning and better examples, reducing errors and improving cross-lingual consistency.
202:This section presents the instruction prompt template. Note that certain locales include supplementary prompts tailored to their linguistic or stylistic characteristics. In-context learning (ICL) examples are omitted here for brevity.

name: Grep
content:
1:## PolyNorm: Few-Shot LLM-Based Text Normalization for Text-to-Speech
17:Building on prior research that focused on English and GPT models, this work introduces PolyNorm, an LLM-assisted TN framework that leverages prompting and contextual learning, and com-
25:Text Normalization (TN) is a key preprocessing step in Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems, converting written forms into their canonical spoken equivalents. Traditional TN systems can exhibit high accuracy, but involve substantial engineering effort, are difficult to scale, and pose challenges to language coverage, particularly in low-resource settings. We propose PolyNorm, a prompt-based approach to TN using Large Language Models (LLMs), aiming to reduce the reliance on manually crafted rules and enable broader linguistic applicability with minimal human intervention. Additionally, we present a language-agnostic pipeline for automatic data curation and evaluation, designed to facilitate scalable experimentation across diverse languages. Experiments across eight languages show consistent reductions in the word error rate (WER) compared to a productiongrade-based system. To support further research, we release PolyNorm-Benchmark, a multilingual data set covering a diverse range of text normalization phenomena.
39:To address these challenges, we use DeepSeekR1 (DeepSeek-AI, 2025) to generate datasets comprising unnormalized and normalized text pairs as our new multilingual benchmark suite, PolyNormBenchmark 2 , designed to ensure consistency across languages and taxonomies. Each dataset spans 27 normalization categories (listed in Table 1) built on Kestrel categories and expanded with new classes, comprising 20 examples per category for a total of 540 high-quality data points per language. For Chinese and Japanese, we define abbreviations as truncated forms or initial-letter substitutions, since conventional definitions may not fully capture their
104:Our prompt design follows a structured, step-bystep and language-agnostic format composed of three components: 1) Instruction Prompt , which defines the normalization task, 2) In-Context Learning Examples , which demonstrate how different types of text should be normalized, and 3) Target Unnormalized Input , to which the model applies learned patterns. PolyNorm uses a standardized English instruction prompt with localized ICL examples tailored to each language's linguistic and stylistic norms.
108:Few-shot prompting with ICL offers a lightweight, scalable solution for multilingual text normalization, relying on high-quality, language-specific examples. The effectiveness of this approach depends on the quality and relevance of the ICL examples provided to the model. We use a unified prompt format across eight languages, varying only the localized examples-except for Japanese, where a supplementary prompt guides katakana output. Our curated ICL set, excluded from PolyNorm-Bench, includes 80-100 high-quality machine-translated Kestrel data and DeepSeek synthetic examples (e.g., favoring 'twenty nineteen' over 'two thou- sand nineteen' for '2019' in English), each normalized and validated by experts to ensure stylistic consistency across domains.
118:All systems were tested on PolyNorm across eight languages listed in Table 2. We report the overall performance with Word Error Rate (WER) and BLEU, which reflects how closely the model's output matches the expected spoken form and relative surface similarity to the reference respectively. For non-whitespace languages (Chinese and Japanese), we report Character Error Rate instead of WER to avoid conflating tokenization errors with true normalization errors. PolyNorm demonstrates substantial advantages in terms of flexibility, coverage, and the ability to handle previously intractable normalization challenges with minimal manual human intervention. Results in Table 2 show improvements in both WER and BLEU across the two LLM systems compared to the rule-based baseline. GPT4o achieves a WER ranging from 4.17% to 7.88% for all languages.
120:We observe that domain-specific constructs, such as website URLs and usernames, which rely on static rules, often pose challenges for tokenization and rule-based normalization due to their inherent unpredictability and rapid evolution. PolyNorm addresses these challenges with the contextual capabilities of LLMs to intelligently segment such expressions. For instance, https://www.mediacityuk.co.uk is normalized as h t t p s colon slash slash media city u k dot co dot u k .
124:Table 2: WER and BLEU scores across 8 languages using PolyNorm-Benchmark. Best scores per row are in bold.
138:Another observation of PolyNorm's context awareness over rule-based systems is its handling of dashes between numbers. In sports scores, a dash is typically verbalized as 'to' (e.g., '3-2' as 'three to two'), whereas in phone numbers it is ignored and the digits are read individually. A rulebased system would require an explicit and often verbose set of context-specific rules to make this distinction, while PolyNorm can infer the correct interpretation directly from context, even in novel or ambiguous formats.
144:Training a reliable TTS system can require millions of high-quality data points. While effective in controlled environments, traditional TN pipelines, heavily rule-based, demand ongoing annotation and patching, often taking months per language and struggling to generalize or scale to noisy, ambiguous, or evolving inputs. PolyNorm replaces this manual loop with an LLM-driven framework using prompt tuning and in-context learning, cutting development overhead and accelerating iteration by refining prompts or examples.
146:Crowdsourcing normalization can be expensive, and it takes weeks of review cycles. PolyNorm, combined with few-shot in-context learning, achieves high grapheme-to-phoneme accuracy with far fewer labeled examples, lowering the barrier for TN in low-resource languages. Beyond cost and efficiency gains, LLM-based TN offers more consistent outputs, better generalization, and language-agnostic deployment, while allowing localized examples to boost performance. By reducing manual effort and speeding iteration, PolyNorm lets developers focus on quality and localization, accelerating research and production.
150:Future work for PolyNorm includes enabling diacritization restoration in languages like Arabic and Hebrew, where diacritics serve critical grammatical and phonetic roles, and missing markers affect grammar and pronunciation. Targeted prompting could improve accuracy and naturalness of speech synthesis. Japanese and other non-whitespace languages could benefit from integrated tokenizers and multitask learning. While normalizing input to Katakana resolves homograph ambiguities (e.g., 〇 read as 'rei,' 'zero,' or 'maru') in Japanese, it may disrupt pitch accent patterns crucial for naturalness. Future work should incorporate suprasegmental features like pitch accent and tone prediction into the normalization pipeline.
154:We present PolyNorm, a multilingual, LLMassisted text normalization system designed to address scalability, cost, and language-coverage limitations of rule-based systems. It achieves strong normalization performance across languages and categories, and significantly reduces WER compared to production-grade rule-based systems, demonstrating its robustness and adaptability.
156:Beyond performance, PolyNorm represents a paradigm shift in how normalization systems can be developed, maintained, and scaled. The use of instruct prompting and in-context learning enables rapid iteration cycles, eliminates the dependency on handcrafted rules, and lowers the entry barrier for low-resource languages and domain-specific applications. Our language-agnostic prompt design and PolyNorm-Benchmark further establish a foundation for standardized evaluation and future research in LLM-based normalization. As we look ahead, expanding our system to handle complex linguistic phenomena such as diacritization, non-whitespace tokenization, and suprasegmental features will further broaden its applicability, making high-quality TTS systems accessible across a wider range of languages and user contexts.
170:LLM-based normalization introduces ethical and safety risks. Because the model learns from large datasets, it may replicate biases in the training data. For example, in gendered languages like Arabic, ambiguous inputs such as 'ant' (which could mean 'you [masc.]' or 'you [fem.]') might be resolved in biased ways. If unaddressed, such biases can reinforce stereotypes or generate inappropriate outputs. Inaccurate normalization may also mislead learners, reinforce incorrect usage, and reduce trust in downstream systems. Therefore, while PolyNorm offers efficient, multilingual normalization, it must be developed with careful attention to data quality, bias mitigation, and user safety to ensure ethical deployment.
174:While PolyNorm demonstrates strong potential in terms of efficiency and multilingual consistency, the model's performance is highly dependent on the quality and representativeness of data used as in-context learning examples. Categories covered in the examples may not capture the full range of normalization needs across different languages and varieties. Furthermore, errors in normalization can propagate into downstream tasks such as translation, sentiment analysis, or educational tools. Although expert-reviewed examples were used for in-context learning, the model may still struggle with edge cases or uncommon linguistic patterns. These limitations must be addressed before considering the system for widespread or critical work.

We report the overall performance with Word Error Rate (WER) and BLEU, which reflects how closely the model's output matches the expected spoken form and relative surface similarity to the reference respectively. For non-whitespace languages (Chinese and Japanese), we report Character Error Rate instead of WER to avoid conflating tokenization errors with true normalization errors. PolyNorm demonstrates substantial advantages in terms of flexibility, coverage, and the ability to handle previously intractable normalization challenges with minimal manual human intervention. Results in Table 2 show improvements in both WER and BLEU across the two LLM systems compared to the rule-based baseline. GPT4o achieves a WER ranging from 4.17% to 7.88% for all languages.

name: Read
content:
1	# PolyNorm
     2	
     3	This dataset accompanies the research paper, [PolyNorm: Few-Shot LLM-Based Text Normalization for Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org).
     4	
     5	
     6	# PolyNorm-Bench: Description
     7	Text Normalization (TN) is a key preprocessing step in Text-to-Speech (TTS) systems, converting written forms into their canonical spoken equivalents. Traditional TN systems can exhibit high accuracy, but involve substantial engineering effort, are difficult to scale, and pose challenges to language coverage, particularly in low-resource settings. We propose PolyNorm, a prompt-based approach to TN using Large Language Models (LLMs), aiming to reduce the reliance on manually crafted rules and enable broader linguistic applicability with minimal human intervention. Additionally, we present a language-agnostic pipeline for automatic data curation and evaluation, designed to facilitate scalable experimentation across diverse languages. Experiments across eight languages showistent reductions in the word error rate (WER) compared to a produproduction-grade-based system. To support further research, we release PolyNorm-Benchmark, a multilingual data set covering a diverse range of text normalization phenomena.
     8	
     9	## Documentation
    10	PolyNorm-Bench is a dataset with normalized and unnormalized data generated by DeepSeek-R1 as foundation across 8 locales, spanning 27 categories. Each synthetic example is edited and verified by internal language experts to ensure the highest standards of linguistic precision, naturalness, and overall quality. The orthography and formatting of synthetic data follow the conventions of target languages. There are 20 examples per category per language, resulting in 540 high-quality data points per language.
    11	
    12	### Normalization Categories
    13	1. Cardinal
    14	2. Date
    15	3. Decimal
    16	4. Ordinal
    17	5. Fraction
    18	6. Time
    19	7. Currency
    20	8. Unit (Measure)
    21	9. Address
    22	10. Acronym/Initialism
    23	11. ISBN
    24	12. Biological Classification
    25	13. Roman Numeral
    26	14. Telephone
    27	15. Sports Score
    28	16. Mathematical Expression
    29	17. Symbol
    30	18. Abbreviation
    31	19. Chemical Formula
    32	20. Legal Reference
    33	21. Vehicle/Product Code
    34	22. Geographic Coordinates
    35	23. Version Number
    36	24. License/Serial Number
    37	25. Musical Notation
    38	26. Stock Ticker
    39	27. Electronic (URL/Email)
    40	
    41	### Languages
    42	American EnglishGerman, French, Mexican Spanish, Italian, Lithuanian, Japanese, and Mandarin Chinese
